speaker: interviewer
text: We're interviewing Helen Rosenblatt, birthname Diament. The interviewer is Martha Frazer. We're in Riverdale, New York in the United States, and the language is English. You were going to tell us why you feel it's so important to give testimony now, to make this tape now.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a very sad story, a very painful one. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I suffered and shared this horrible fate with many, mostly my relatives, friends, and people like myself, whose only crime was that they were born Jews. This is a story of great injustice, of crying for help. Unfortunately, the world around was silent. It was the cruel work of human beasts who lost their sense of morality and decency, and decided to solve their inner problems by finding a scapegoat, the Jews. Their plan was the Final Solution, their modern, cruel technique to kill, murder, and burn every man, woman, and even innocent babies. They killed six million too many. Only a small number of great Jewish communities survived. I survived to bear witness for all those innocent Jews who cried, suffered, and died, but could not tell the world of their pains. After a long silence, some nights of nightmares of the past, I decided to speak about the Holocaust. It's my obligation as a survivor to leave you the legacy of remembrance of telling this tragic chapter to the next generation. 'Don't be afraid of your past so that it shouldn't happen again.' Those words by an American philosopher, Santayana, are engraved at the entrance of Dachau concentration camp. Let's pray it will never happen again. And these are the three reasons why I decided to tell this story. One, I am a witness to those atrocities. Two, I am an educator. The teacher's role is to bring this historical knowledge to the young generation. Three, in my silent prayers for survival, I made a silent promise, [HEBREW] in Hebrew, dedicated to the memory of my dear parents, that I will talk about their pains and suffering. I keep this holy promise.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is my grandfather, the only picture I have of my family. When we walked out from our house, I didn't take any pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: How did I get this one? A cousin of ours who lived in Israel before the war started, and he lives now in Israel, gave me this picture. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the document my husband got when he left Czestochowa to survive as a Pole under a different name. This kind of paper is called Kennkarte. At this time, his name was Pikuba Wladystaw. [PAUSES FOR 15 SECONDS] This is the picture taken right after the liberation, when I told you in my story that I organized a school for some teenager survivors. I am standing on the left side at the very back, a skinny person-- that's me. The rest are students. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is a picture taken in [INAUDIBLE] next to Frankfurt, where we organized a high school for survivors. I am here at the right side in a checkered dress. The rest are students. And two teachers-- next to me one teacher, and the woman in the middle another. The rest are students. Above you see the name of the group. It's in German, it's in Hebrew, and it is in English. High School for Adults. [INAUDIBLE] sign. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is a copy of a writing made at the time of my retirement. Somebody from the local school came to interview me, and you'll see in the picture on the right side, I'm standing with my first-graders, explaining to them in Hebrew something that they learned about trees. On the bottom is my picture. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] This is a picture of my family. They attended the goodbye party at my retirement. On the left side is my sister Lucy. Next to her is my husband. I am the next one. On the right side is my son Steve. Those are my lovely grandchildren. They live in Houston, Texas. My son Steve is a very successful lawyer. Those two kids-- the picture taken a few years ago. Right now, this is Nathan on the right side. He is older. He is now 11 and a half. On the left side is James, whose birthday, the tenth birthday, is this Saturday. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They go to school. Smart kids. We are planning to visit them. We love them so much.
